required.title: [For Student and Mentor] Exemplars relevant to the student's current work

competence_markers.description: They define specific quality criteria before beginning complex tasks, without prompting
They can study an exemplar and articulate specific qualities that make it excellent
They produce revised work that is demonstrably better than the first version, and can explain what improved
They accept correction with decreasing defensiveness — correction is received as information, not as attack
They can compare current work to older work and accurately identify improvement and stagnation
They self-assess before submitting work — the "quality pause" is becoming habitual
They seek feedback rather than avoiding it — they want to know where the gap is
Their correction log shows changing patterns — new corrections rather than repeated ones, indicating real skill growth
They can distinguish between "adequate" and "excellent" in their own work and in others'
They can articulate the difference between perfectionism (anxiety-driven) and standards (quality-driven)

narration_prompts.prompt: What does "done" mean for a piece of writing?
narration_prompts.detail: Not just "I finished writing." What are the actual criteria for a piece of writing being done well?

narration_prompts.prompt: Describe an exemplar you have studied recently.
narration_prompts.detail: What was it? What made it excellent? What specific quality did you notice that your own work does not yet have?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about something you revised.
narration_prompts.detail: What was wrong with the first version? What did you change? Is the second version better? How specifically?

narration_prompts.prompt: How do you respond when someone tells you your work is not good enough?
narration_prompts.detail: Be honest. What is your first reaction? What do you do after that reaction?

narration_prompts.prompt: Compare something you made recently to something you made three months ago.
narration_prompts.detail: What is better? What is the same? How do you know?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between perfectionism and having standards?
narration_prompts.detail: When does caring about quality become harmful? How do you know you have crossed that line?

cross_connections.description: The trades are the most tangible domain for IC2.2-B. A joint fits or it does not. A measurement is accurate or it is not. Exemplars are physical and visible. The gap between your work and the standard is measurable in fractions of an inch. Trade work teaches quality assessment in the most direct way possible.

Artistic standards develop through the same process: exemplar study, deliberate revision, honest self-assessment. IC2.2-B gives the student the vocabulary and framework for improving creative work, not just producing it.

Technique standards in physical performance are IC2.2 applied to the body. A pull-up done with poor form is not a pull-up done to standard, regardless of whether you got your chin over the bar. The same quality question applies: is this actually done right, or did I just get through it?
Quality work requires time for revision. The planning skills from IC1.1-B are what make revision possible — you must plan time for a second draft, not discover after the fact that you have none.

Quality work requires sustained attention. Sloppy work is often the product of fragmented attention — doing the task while thinking about three other things. IC2.3 builds the focus capacity that IC2.2 requires.

Credibility as a leader depends on demonstrated personal standards. The student who holds themselves to quality in their own work earns the authority to hold others to quality in theirs. Leadership without personal standards is hypocrisy.

content.why_this_matters:
In Stretching Branches, you learned a simple rule: do it right. You learned what "done" looks like for basic tasks — a made bed, a clean workspace, tools put away. You learned to redo work that was not finished. You learned the difference between done and stopped.

Now the question gets harder.

"Done" for a made bed is straightforward. You can see it. But what about a piece of writing — when is that "done"? What about a drawing, a math solution, a woodworking project, a presentation? These are not binary — not just done or not-done. They exist on a spectrum from poor to adequate to good to excellent. And most people park somewhere around "adequate" and call it done. Not because they cannot do better. Because doing better requires something they have not yet developed: the ability to define what "better" looks like, compare their work to that definition, and close the gap deliberately.

That is what this packet builds.

You are going to learn to define "done" for complex tasks — not just "is it finished?" but "is it good?" You will learn to compare your work to exemplars — examples of excellent work in whatever you are doing. You will learn to revise, to produce a second version that is better than the first on purpose. You will learn to accept correction without defensiveness, because correction is just information about the distance between where you are and where the standard is. And you will learn to measure your own improvement over time, so you can see the trajectory you are on.

The core question remains the same: **"Is this actually done, or did I stop because I got tired?"** But now the question has more depth. It is not just asking whether you completed the task. It is asking whether you completed it well. Whether you pushed past the point where it was easy and into the territory where actual quality lives.

Most people never learn to ask that question honestly. They produce work that is fine — acceptable, passable, good enough — and they stop there. Not because they are lazy. Because no one ever showed them what excellent looked like, or taught them how to close the gap between their work and that standard. You are going to learn that now.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Defining "done" for complex tasks** — articulating specific quality criteria before beginning, not just "is it finished?"
2. **Comparison to exemplars** — studying what excellent work actually looks like in the domain you are working in
3. **Deliberate revision** — producing a second (or third) version that is measurably better than the first
4. **Accepting correction without defensiveness** — understanding that feedback is information about the gap, not an attack on your person
5. **Measuring improvement over time** — tracking whether your work is getting better, and by what specific measures
6. **The distinction between adequate and excellent** — developing an internal sense for the spectrum of quality
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Defining "Done" — Beyond the Binary

In Stretching Branches, "done" was mostly binary. The bed is made or it is not. The blocks are in the bin or they are not. You could see the answer.

Now the work is more complex, and "done" is no longer obvious. Consider:

**A piece of writing.** When is an essay done? When you have written something? When you have filled the page? When you have said what you meant to say? When you have said it clearly? When you have said it clearly and compellingly? When a reader could follow your argument without getting lost? When the grammar is correct? When the word choices are precise? When the opening grabs attention and the conclusion lands with force?

Each of those is a different standard. And most people stop at the first one: "I wrote something." They call it done because they have words on the page. But words on a page is not done. Words on a page is a first draft — the raw material from which something good might eventually be built.

**A woodworking project.** When is a shelf done? When it is assembled? When it is level? When the joints are tight? When the surface is sanded smooth? When the finish is even? When it will hold the weight it is designed to hold, for years, without failing?

**A math solution.** When is it done? When you have an answer? When you have checked the answer? When you can explain how you got there? When you have verified that your method works for similar problems? When you understand why the method works, not just that it works?

**The principle:** For any complex task, you must define what "done" means before you begin. Not vaguely — specifically. What are the criteria? What would a more experienced person look at to judge whether this is finished? Write these criteria down. Then, when you think you are done, check your work against the list. Is each criterion met? If yes, you are done. If no, you know exactly what remains.

This is not natural. Your instinct will be to declare something done the moment the effort becomes uncomfortable. The criteria list is your defense against that instinct.

### Part 2: Exemplars — What Excellent Actually Looks Like

You cannot produce excellent work if you have never seen excellent work. You cannot aim at a target you cannot see.

An **exemplar** is an example of excellent work in the domain you are working in. Not perfect work — no work is perfect. But work that represents a standard significantly above your current level. Work that, when you study it, teaches you what quality looks like in this specific area.

**How to use exemplars:**

1. **Find them.** Before you begin a task, look at examples of that task done well. If you are writing an essay, read excellent essays. If you are building a shelf, look at well-built shelves. If you are drawing, study drawings by people better than you. Your mentor can help you find these.

2. **Study them.** Do not just glance. Look carefully. What makes this good? Be specific. In an essay: the opening is clear. The argument builds logically. The sentences are varied. The conclusion does not just repeat the introduction. In a shelf: the joints are tight. The surfaces are smooth. The proportions look right. The finish is even.

3. **Identify the gap.** Now look at your own work. What is the distance between your work and the exemplar? Be specific. Not "mine is worse." That is useless. "My opening is confusing where theirs is clear." "My joints have gaps where theirs are tight." "My sentences all start the same way." Specific gaps give you specific things to fix.

4. **Close the gap.** This is the work. Not all at once — you will not go from where you are to where the exemplar is in one revision. But you can close part of the gap. Pick the most significant difference and address it. Then the next one. Over time, over many projects, the gap narrows.

**Exemplars are not meant to discourage.** If you look at excellent work and feel hopeless, the exemplar was chosen at the wrong level. The right exemplar is above your current ability but within your visible horizon — you can see how you might get there, even if it would take significant work. Your mentor should help calibrate this.

### Part 3: The Art of Revision — Making It Better on Purpose

A first draft is not a finished product. It is a starting point.

This is one of the most important things you will learn in this packet, and it goes against every instinct you have. Your instinct says: I did it. I am done. Moving on. But the truth is that almost nothing is good on the first attempt. Writing is not good on the first draft. Drawings are not good on the first sketch. Projects are not good on the first build. The first attempt reveals problems. The second attempt fixes some of them. The third attempt fixes more. Quality is produced by revision, not by inspiration.

**The revision process:**

1. **Complete the first attempt.** Do not revise while you work — finish the thing first. Get it to a state where it exists as a complete (even if rough) whole.

2. **Step away.** If possible, put distance between yourself and the work. An hour. A day. The distance gives you fresh eyes.

3. **Return and assess.** Look at the work with the same eyes you used to study the exemplar. What is good? What is weak? Where is the gap between what you produced and what you know quality looks like?

4. **Revise with specific targets.** Do not just "make it better." Identify two or three specific things to improve, and improve them. In writing: clarify this paragraph, strengthen this opening, fix these sentence patterns. In a project: tighten this joint, smooth this surface, correct this measurement.

5. **Assess again.** Is it better? Is it done — really done, by the criteria you defined? If yes, stop. If no, repeat.

**You are not expected to revise everything.** A math problem does not usually need a second draft. A chore does not need a revision cycle. Revision applies to work that has depth — writing, projects, creative work, skill-building exercises. Your mentor will help you know when revision is expected and when single-pass completion is sufficient.

### Part 4: Accepting Correction Without Defensiveness

Someone is going to look at your work and tell you it is not good enough. This is going to happen repeatedly. Your response to this moment is one of the most important skills you will develop.

**What correction actually is:** Information. That is all. When someone says "this paragraph is unclear" or "this joint is loose" or "your math is wrong on step three," they are telling you the location and nature of the gap between your work and the standard. This is useful information. It tells you exactly what to fix.

**What correction is not:** An attack on you as a person. It is not saying you are bad, stupid, or incapable. It is saying the work is not there yet. You and your work are not the same thing. Your identity is not your essay. Your self-worth is not your shelf. The ability to separate yourself from your work — to hear "this is not good enough yet" without hearing "you are not good enough" — is one of the most valuable capacities a person can develop.

**How to receive correction well:**
1. **Listen.** Do not formulate your defense while the other person is talking. Just listen.
2. **Understand.** Make sure you know what the gap is. Ask questions if you do not understand: "Can you show me what you mean?" or "What would better look like here?"
3. **Do not argue.** Not yet. First, consider whether the correction is accurate. In most cases, it is — especially when it comes from someone more experienced than you. The impulse to argue is usually ego defense, not legitimate disagreement.
4. **Fix it.** Apply the correction. See if the work is better.
5. **Learn from it.** The correction is not just about this one piece of work. It is about the pattern. If your paragraphs are always unclear, that is a pattern to work on. If your joints are always loose, that is a technique to develop. Each correction is a data point about where your skills need growth.

**The person who accepts correction well improves faster than everyone else.** This is not opinion — it is observable. The student who gets defensive spends energy protecting their ego. The student who listens and fixes spends energy improving their work. Over months and years, the gap between them becomes enormous.

### Part 5: Measuring Improvement Over Time

How do you know if you are getting better? Not by feeling — feelings are unreliable. By measurement.

**The portfolio method:** Keep examples of your work over time. Writing samples from each month. Photos of projects. Drawings. Recordings of performances. Date everything. Then, periodically, go back and compare. Look at what you produced three months ago and what you produced this week. Is there a difference? Can you name the specific ways the newer work is better?

**The criteria method:** When you define "done" for a task, you create criteria. Track your performance against those criteria over time. If your writing criteria include "clear opening paragraph" and three months ago you rarely achieved that but now you usually do, that is measurable improvement.

**The feedback method:** Track the corrections you receive. Are you getting the same correction repeatedly (indicating you have not improved in that area)? Or are the corrections changing (indicating you have fixed old problems and are now encountering new, higher-level ones)? New problems are progress. Same problems are stagnation.

**Why this matters:** Without measurement, you are guessing about your own trajectory. Guessing tends to go one of two ways: you think you are better than you are (which kills motivation to improve), or you think you are worse than you are (which kills confidence). Neither is useful. Data is useful. Look at the work. Compare it to older work. Let the evidence tell you the truth.
content.formative_practices:
### Practice 1: Define "Done" Before You Begin [For Every Significant Task]
Before beginning any complex task — a writing assignment, a project, a skill practice session — write down 3-5 specific criteria for "done." Not "make it good." Specific: "The opening paragraph states the main argument. Each body paragraph has a clear topic sentence. The conclusion does not just restate the introduction." Check your work against these criteria when you think you are finished.

### Practice 2: Exemplar Study [Weekly]
At least once a week, study an exemplar in whatever domain you are currently working in. If you are writing, read excellent writing. If you are building, examine excellent building. Spend 15-20 minutes. Write down three specific things that make the exemplar good. Then identify one of those qualities you can work on in your own next piece.

### Practice 3: The Two-Draft Minimum [For All Writing and Major Projects]
For any writing assignment or major project, produce at least two versions. The first draft is completed, then set aside. The second draft addresses specific weaknesses identified in the first. The student should be able to articulate what is better about the second version and why.

### Practice 4: Correction Log [Ongoing]
Keep a simple log of corrections received — from mentors, from peers, from self-assessment. Note the date, the work, and the specific correction. Review the log monthly. Patterns will emerge. Repeated corrections identify skill gaps that need focused work. New and different corrections indicate progress.

### Practice 5: Portfolio Comparison [Monthly]
Once a month, pull out work from 1-3 months ago and compare it to recent work. Be specific: what is better? What is the same? What has gotten worse? Write a brief (3-5 sentence) assessment. Share it with your mentor.

### Practice 6: The Quality Pause [After Every Task]
Before declaring any task done, pause. Look at it. Ask: "Is this actually done, or did I stop because I got tired? Would I be willing to show this to someone whose opinion I respect? Does it meet the criteria I set?" If the answer to any of these is no, identify what needs to change, and change it.

### Practice 7: Peer Exchange [Monthly]
At least once a month, exchange work with a peer, sibling, or fellow student. Each person identifies one thing the other's work does well and one specific thing that could be improved. This is practice in both giving and receiving honest feedback — both skills are necessary for IC2.2-B.

mentor_guide.formation_environment:
### Providing Exemplars

The student cannot aim at a target they cannot see. Before they can assess the quality of their own work, they need to know what quality looks like. This is your responsibility.

**Principles for exemplar selection:**

1. **Domain-specific.** The exemplar must be in the same domain as the student's work. An excellent essay is not an exemplar for a woodworking project. Find quality in the specific area the student is working in.

2. **Appropriately aspirational.** The exemplar should be above the student's current level but within their visible horizon. An 11-year-old looking at a Pulitzer-winning essay will feel despair, not aspiration. An 11-year-old looking at an excellent essay by a 14-year-old will think: "I could get there." Calibrate the distance.

3. **Annotated, not just shown.** Do not just hand the student an exemplar and say "make yours like this." Walk through it together. What makes this good? Be specific. "Notice how the first sentence makes you want to read the next one." "Look at this joint — see how there is no gap? That is what a tight joint looks like." The student needs to learn to see quality, and you teach them to see it by pointing at specific things.

4. **Varied over time.** Different exemplars for different tasks. Different exemplars as the student grows. The target should always be slightly ahead of where they are — close enough to be motivating, far enough to require real effort.

### Teaching Revision

Most students resist revision because they have been taught (implicitly, by the culture) that good work comes out right the first time. If it needs to be redone, that means they failed. This belief is both false and destructive.

**Your job is to normalize revision.**

- Share your own revision process. "Here is the first draft of something I wrote. Here is the final version. See the difference? That difference is the work." If you do not write, build, or create anything yourself, this is a problem — you cannot mentor quality you do not practice.
- Frame the first draft explicitly as a starting point, not a finished product. "Good. Now you have material to work with. Let us look at what needs to improve."
- Never accept a first draft as final when the work has depth. Writing, projects, creative work — the revision expectation is non-negotiable. The student may push back: "But I am done!" Your response: "You have a first draft. Now make it better. Here is what I see that could improve." This is not cruelty. It is the actual process by which quality is produced.
- Teach the student to revise with specific targets, not just "make it better." Vague revision instructions produce vague revision. "Strengthen your opening paragraph so the reader knows your argument in the first three sentences" produces specific improvement.

### Delivering Correction That Can Be Heard

Correction is the mechanism by which the gap between current work and the standard becomes visible to the student. If the student cannot hear the correction — because it triggers defensiveness, shame, or shutdown — the mechanism breaks.

**Principles:**

1. **Separate the person from the work.** Always. "This paragraph is unclear" is about the paragraph. "You are a bad writer" is about the person. The first is correction. The second is damage. Never conflate the two.

2. **Be specific.** "This is not good" is useless. "This joint has a visible gap — the saw cut was off by about 1/16 of an inch" is useful. The student needs to know what is wrong and where, so they know what to fix.

3. **Name what works before naming what does not.** Not as flattery — as calibration. "Your argument is structured well. The evidence in paragraph three is strong. The opening is weak — a reader would not know what you are arguing until paragraph two." The student now knows what to protect (the structure, paragraph three) and what to fix (the opening). This is more useful than "the opening is bad."

4. **Deliver correction in proportion to the work.** A minor task does not need extensive critique. A major project deserves detailed feedback. Over-correcting small things produces a student who feels nothing is ever good enough. Under-correcting major work produces a student who does not know what quality looks like.

5. **Watch the student's response.** If they shut down, you have pushed too hard or delivered the correction without enough safety. If they argue, they are in ego defense — let them settle, then return to the specific point. If they listen and ask questions, the correction is landing. Adjust your approach based on what you observe.

### Managing Defensiveness

Defensiveness is the primary obstacle to quality development in Weathering Storms. The student who cannot hear correction cannot improve.

**Defensiveness is normal.** Especially in this age range (8-14), where identity is forming and everything feels personal. Telling a 12-year-old that their essay needs work can feel, to the 12-year-old, like telling them they are stupid. This is not rational, but it is real.

**How to work with it:**

1. **Do not match it.** If the student gets defensive, do not escalate. Stay calm. State the observation factually. "I understand this is frustrating. The paragraph is still unclear. Let us work on it."

2. **Name the pattern when it becomes habitual.** "I notice that when I point out something that needs improvement, you argue before you consider it. I want you to try something: listen first. Think about whether it is accurate. Then respond."

3. **Celebrate the moments when they do not get defensive.** Not with praise for meeting a baseline — with genuine acknowledgment. "You heard that correction and went straight to fixing it. That is a skill most adults do not have."

4. **Track it over time.** Defensiveness should decrease as the student matures and as they build confidence through demonstrated improvement. If it is increasing or not changing, something in the dynamic needs to shift — possibly the ratio of correction to acknowledgment, possibly the way correction is being delivered, possibly the student's overall confidence level.
mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Defines Quality Before Beginning

**Developing well:** The student articulates criteria for "done" before beginning complex tasks. They do this without prompting, or with minimal prompting. The criteria are specific and measurable, not vague.

**Developing slowly:** The student starts tasks without considering what quality means. When asked "what does done look like for this?" they cannot answer, or give vague responses: "good" or "finished."

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you modeled this? Have you shown the student how you define quality criteria for your own work? Is the student being given tasks where quality criteria exist and can be articulated? Start by defining criteria together, then gradually shift to the student defining them independently.

### 2. Uses Exemplars Effectively

**Developing well:** The student studies exemplars and can name specific qualities that make them excellent. They can identify the gap between the exemplar and their own work in specific terms.

**Developing slowly:** The student glances at exemplars without engagement. They cannot articulate what makes the exemplar good, or they say "it is just better" without specifics.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are the exemplars at the right level? (Too far above = despair, too close = no aspiration.) Are you studying the exemplar with the student and pointing out specific qualities, or just handing it to them? Has the student been taught how to study an exemplar analytically?

### 3. Revises Deliberately

**Developing well:** The student produces revised work that is demonstrably better than the first version. They can explain what they changed and why. The revision addresses specific weaknesses, not just surface-level changes.

**Developing slowly:** The student's "revision" is cosmetic — they change a few words, fix spelling, but do not address structural or substantive weaknesses. Or they refuse to revise at all.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are your revision instructions specific enough? ("Make it better" produces nothing. "Strengthen the opening so the argument is clear in the first two sentences" produces improvement.) Is the student given enough time for revision, or is revision squeezed in as an afterthought?

### 4. Accepts Correction Productively

**Developing well:** Correction is received without significant defensiveness. The student listens, considers, and applies the feedback. They may ask clarifying questions. They do not argue as a first response.

**Developing slowly:** Every correction produces defensiveness — arguing, shutting down, crying, or dismissing the feedback. The student's work does not improve because the correction is not being absorbed.

**Critical distinction:** Some students accept correction gracefully but never apply it. Politeness is not the marker — application is. Does the work actually improve based on the correction?

### 5. Measures Own Improvement

**Developing well:** The student can compare recent work to older work and accurately identify what has improved, what has stagnated, and what has regressed. They do this with specifics, not feelings.

**Developing slowly:** The student has no awareness of their own trajectory. They cannot tell you whether they are better at writing than they were three months ago, or what specifically has improved.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the portfolio being maintained? Are monthly comparisons happening? Have you modeled this by showing the student your own improvement (or the improvement arc of someone they respect)?

### 6. Self-Assesses Before Submitting

**Developing well:** The student pauses before declaring work done and evaluates it against their own criteria. They catch some gaps before you do. They sometimes decide, on their own, that something needs another round of work.

**Developing slowly:** The student submits work the moment they stop working on it, without any quality check. They are surprised when gaps are identified.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the "quality pause" being practiced? Is it built into the routine, or left to willpower? Try making it structural: "Before you bring this to me, write down one thing that is strong and one thing that is weak."
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Accepting "Good Enough"
The most common Weathering Storms failure is accepting work that is adequate when it could be good. The student produces something that is fine — not terrible, not embarrassing, but clearly not their best effort. And you accept it because the battle of requiring revision is exhausting. Every time you do this, you teach the student that adequate is the real standard, regardless of what you say.

The fix: require revision on meaningful work. Not everything — not math drill answers or daily chores. But writing, projects, creative work, skill demonstrations — these should reflect genuine effort toward quality, and a first attempt that clearly parks at "adequate" should be sent back with specific targets.

### Over-Correction Leading to Perfectionism
The opposite failure: correcting so much that the student becomes afraid to produce anything because it will never be good enough. The student who stares at a blank page for an hour because they are afraid the first sentence will not be perfect is not demonstrating standards. They are demonstrating anxiety.

Watch for signs: excessive time spent on minor details while avoiding the substance. Inability to start. Distress out of proportion to the task. Repeated self-criticism that sounds more like self-attack than self-assessment. If you see these, back off the correction intensity and emphasize the acceptability of rough first drafts. The revision process exists precisely so that the first attempt does not need to be perfect.

### Correcting Without Teaching
Pointing out that something is wrong without teaching how to fix it produces frustration, not improvement. "This is unclear" is not enough. "This is unclear — I think the problem is that you have two ideas in one paragraph. Try separating them and see if each becomes clearer on its own." The correction includes the instruction. The student learns the principle, not just the specific fix.

### Comparing Students to Each Other
"Your sister's essay was much better at your age" destroys IC2.2 development. The student's comparison should always be to the standard and to their own trajectory — never to a sibling, peer, or other student. Quality is personal: the question is whether this student is improving, not whether they are better or worse than someone else.

### Neglecting the Emotional Dimension
Quality development is emotional, not just cognitive. The student is learning to look at their own work honestly, which requires courage. They are learning to accept that their work is not yet as good as it could be, which requires humility. They are learning to revise, which requires patience. If you treat quality as a purely technical matter and ignore the emotional demands, the student will either shut down or become mechanical about quality — doing the revision because it is required, without developing the internal drive that makes quality self-sustaining.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: What "Done" Means for Writing
**Strong:** Multiple specific criteria. "The argument is clear. The evidence supports it. The opening makes the reader want to continue. The sentences are not all the same length. The conclusion adds something new." Shows internalized quality framework.
**Weak:** "When I finish writing" or "when it looks right." No criteria. The student has not developed a quality vocabulary for this domain.

### Prompt 2: Exemplar Study
**Strong:** Names a specific exemplar. Identifies specific qualities with analysis. "I read an essay about exploration, and the opening sentence asked a question that made me want to know the answer. My essays start with 'In this essay I will...' which is boring." Shows analytical engagement and self-aware gap identification.
**Weak:** "It was good." No specifics. No connection to their own work.

### Prompt 3: Revision Experience
**Strong:** Describes the specific weaknesses of the first version and the specific improvements in the second. Acknowledges that the revision was work but that the result was better. "My first draft had a confusing middle section. I restructured it so each paragraph covered one idea. Now you can follow it."
**Weak:** "I fixed the spelling" or "I made it longer." Surface-level revision that does not address substance.

### Prompt 4: Response to Correction
**Strong:** Honest self-assessment. "My first reaction is to argue. I am working on listening first." Or: "It depends on how it is said. When it is specific, I can use it. When it is vague, I get frustrated." Shows self-awareness about the defensiveness pattern and active effort to manage it.
**Weak:** "I do not get upset" (when they clearly do). Or: "It makes me mad because they are wrong." No self-awareness, no willingness to consider that the correction might be accurate.

### Prompt 5: Improvement Over Time
**Strong:** Specific comparison with evidence. "My writing three months ago had run-on sentences everywhere. Now I catch most of them. My openings are still weak — that is what I am working on." Uses portfolio evidence, not just feelings.
**Weak:** "I am better" (with no specifics) or "I am not better" (self-defeating, without evidence). Cannot point to specific improvements or specific areas that need work.

### Prompt 6: Perfectionism vs. Standards
**Strong:** Clear distinction. "Standards mean you know what done looks like and you work until you get there. Perfectionism means you never feel like anything is good enough and you get stuck." Can identify which pattern they tend toward. Shows understanding that the goal is quality, not paralysis.
**Weak:** Cannot distinguish between the two. Or: "I am a perfectionist" worn as a badge, without recognizing that perfectionism is a failure mode, not a virtue.
